title: Middle English Humorous Tales in Verse (encoded selection)
summary: The repository XML is a local copy/version of U-M item AGD2855.0001.001, an uncorrected OCR/Level 1 TEI encoding of the title page and four-work selection on pages 1–59 of George H. McKnight's 1913 Middle English Humorous Tales in Verse. McKnight identifies Bodleian MS Digby 86 as the textual base for Dame Siriz and The Fox and Wolf in the Well, Bodleian MS Ashmole 61 as the base for Sir Cleges, and current NLS Adv.MS.19.1.11 as the source of Sir Cleges lines 40–42 and 130–132. He says that he collated the proofs against separate rotographic copies of those three witnesses, but does not identify the continuous transcription or compositor-copy route; no direct edition-to-rotograph or edition-to-codex transcription is asserted. He printed the Interludium de clerico et puella in a form closely dependent on Wilhelm Heuser's 1907 manuscript-based edition. Treichel's prior Sir Cleges parallel edition reaches Ashmole 61 through Max Kaluza's transcript checked by Karl D. Bülbring, while its Edinburgh column revises Henry Weber's 1810 manuscript edition through an unidentified correction route. The Interludium's unique British Library roll witness, Add MS 23986, is currently recorded as mislaid; a cataloged 2026 microfilm is requestable. Complete public facsimiles of Digby 86, Ashmole 61, and Adv.MS.19.1.11, plus complete public carriers for the used printed editions, are mapped to exact work-bearing portions and cached privately in full.
review_notes: The four medieval works, printed editions, physical witnesses, working transcripts, catalogs, photographic surrogates, modern digital encodings, supporting scholarship, and repository artifact are separate graph layers.
McKnight's rotographs are proof-collation sources only on the available method statement; the continuous transcript or compositor copy for the three principal texts remains unresolved.

Treichel's Oxford and Edinburgh columns have asymmetric transmission paths: only Oxford is routed through the Kaluza/Bülbring working transcript; Edinburgh is routed through Weber's 1810 print with a separately supported manuscript-comparison edge of unidentified method.
Weber's bracketed printed lines 533–540 are a modern editorial supplement and are excluded from every manuscript-transcription scope.

The NLS catalog expression and Treichel's German-only dissertation issue are modeled separately from the physical witness, image facsimile, and journal edition that actually prints the Middle English text.
Dame Siriz and the Interludium are related analogues, but no direct derivation relation between their surviving texts is asserted.
Rights statements apply only to the named entity or component. Public-domain medieval text does not make modern manuscript photography unrestricted.
The ignored source cache retains whole manuscripts and complete printed source objects where available; local work_portion locators identify the relevant portions without implying cropping.

The source XML is 98,939 bytes at the pinned CME revision.

notes: The schema has no abstract-work entity type; catalog_description keeps the intellectual work visible without conflating it with a catalog record.

entities.label: McKnight 1913 Middle English Humorous Tales in Verse

bibliographic.citation: George H. McKnight, ed., Middle English Humorous Tales in Verse (Boston and London: D. C. Heath & Co., 1913), lxxv + 156 pp. and folded plate.
bibliographic.title: Middle English Humorous Tales in Verse
bibliographic.series: The Belles-Lettres Series, Section II: Middle English Literature

notes: The encoded selection is the book title page plus Dame Siriz on pp. 1–20, Interludium de clerico et puella on pp. 21–24, The Fox and Wolf in the Well on pp. 25–37, and Sir Cleges on pp. 38–59.
The complete book also contains a preface, introduction, bibliography, explanatory notes, glossary, and other matter not textually encoded in the repository XML.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of McKnight 1913
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 244-page scan with PDF, OCR, and scan metadata derivatives

entities.label: Internet Archive OCR derivatives for McKnight 1913
Rotographic copy of MS Digby 86 used by McKnight
entities.description: The Digby photographic working copy against which McKnight reports collating proofs for Dame Siriz and The Fox and Wolf in the Well. Its exact production, extent, present location, and survival are unknown; it is not established as the continuous transcription source.

entities.label: Rotographic copy of MS Ashmole 61 used by McKnight
entities.description: The Ashmole photographic working copy against which McKnight reports collating Sir Cleges proofs. Its exact production, extent, present location, and survival are unknown; it is not established as the continuous transcription source.

entities.label: Rotographic copy of the Edinburgh Sir Cleges witness used by McKnight
entities.description: The Edinburgh photographic working copy against which McKnight reports collating Sir Cleges proofs. His source statement separately identifies six supplied Edinburgh lines, but does not establish this rotograph as their continuous-copy source. Its exact production, extent, present location, and survival are unknown.

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Digby 86
entities.description: The unique witness used for Dame Siriz at ff. 165r–168r and The Fox and Wolf in the Well at ff. 138r–140r.

notes: Produced in Worcestershire; witness production place is not asserted as either work's composition place.

entities.label: Bodleian Medieval Manuscripts catalog description of MS Digby 86
Digital Bodleian complete facsimile of MS Digby 86
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 / 430 color JPEG access derivatives

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS Ashmole 61
entities.description: McKnight's base witness for Sir Cleges at ff. 67v–73r; the work ends with Amen partway down f. 73r, where the next text begins on the same surface.

notes: Written by the scribe Rate/Rathe, probably in England and perhaps Leicester; this is not treated as a composition localization.

entities.label: Bodleian Medieval Manuscripts catalog description of MS Ashmole 61
Digital Bodleian complete exposed image sequence for MS Ashmole 61
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 / 358 color JPEG access derivatives

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.19.1.11
entities.description: The second Sir Cleges witness at ff. 71r–79v. Its final work-bearing leaf is missing, so the text breaks after line 544/545. McKnight supplies six lines in his Ashmole-based edition from this witness.

notes: The catalog records the last Sir Cleges leaf, carrying line 545 onward, as missing.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland catalog description of Adv.MS.19.1.11
entities.description: The current NLS Archives and Manuscripts catalog expression for the physical codex, including its shelfmark, extent, Sir Cleges locus, missing terminal work leaf, and microfilm references.
notes: This modern catalog expression is separate from both the medieval codex and the NLS photographic facsimile.

entities.label: NLS complete digital facsimile of Adv.MS.19.1.11
entities.reproduction_format: 370-page color PDF and IIIF Presentation 2 sequence

entities.label: British Library, Add MS 23986
entities.description: A small vellum roll bearing two texts on opposite sides in different hands. The verso contains the unique, imperfect 84-line Interludium de clerico et puella.

date_statements.label: catalog date
date_statements.value: reign of Edward I / late thirteenth century

holding.notes: The current catalog marks the original as mislaid and records MICROFILM 2026 AVAILABLE.

notes: Mislaid is modeled as missing, not destroyed or definitively lost.
No public digital image of the roll was found; the cataloged 2026 microfilm is the current reproduction lead.

entities.label: British Library catalog description of Add MS 23986
Heuser 1907 Interludium and Dame Siriz study

bibliographic.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, ‘Das Interludium de Clerico et Puella und das Fabliau von Dame Siriz,’ Anglia 30 (1907): 306–319.
bibliographic.title: Das Interludium de Clerico et Puella und das Fabliau von Dame Siriz

notes: Heuser states that he prints the Interludium anew from the manuscript, correcting Wright, and gives 84 lines.
He argues that Dame Siriz and the Interludium share an older dramatic source but that neither surviving text derives directly from the other.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Anglia 30 (1907)
Internet Archive OCR for Anglia 30 (1907)
Weber 1810 Sir Cleges edition

bibliographic.citation: Henry Weber, ed., Metrical Romances of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Centuries, vol. 1 (Edinburgh: George Ramsay and Company for Archibald Constable and Co., 1810), 329–354, 381.
bibliographic.title: Metrical Romances of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Centuries
bibliographic.publisher: George Ramsay and Company for Archibald Constable and Co.

notes: Weber says that the Advocates' Library manuscript was the only copy known to him and prints Sir Cleges from it.
Printed lines 533–540 are Weber's bracketed modern supplement to what he thought was a short terminal defect; they are not transcribed from the surviving medieval witness.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Weber's Metrical Romances volume 1 (1810)
entities.reproduction_format: Complete 480-page PDF with OCR and scan-metadata derivatives

entities.label: Internet Archive OCR text for Weber's Metrical Romances volume 1 (1810)
Kaluza's working transcript of the Ashmole Sir Cleges
entities.description: The working transcript of MS Ashmole 61 that Max Kaluza prepared and Karl D. Bülbring subsequently compared again with the original manuscript for Treichel's Oxford column.
notes: Kaluza is credited as maker of the transcript; Bülbring is credited only for comparing that transcript again with the original. The generic creator_ids field records their distinct material responsibility, not joint transcription.

manuscript_witness is used as the closest physical-carrier workaround because the current schema has no dedicated working-transcript entity type; this is a modern editorial transcript, not a medieval witness.
No present location or surviving physical copy of the working transcript was established.

entities.label: Treichel 1896 Sir Cleges parallel edition and study

bibliographic.citation: Adolf Treichel, ‘Sir Cleges. Eine mittelenglische Romanze,’ Englische Studien 22 (1896): 345–389.
bibliographic.title: Sir Cleges. Eine mittelenglische Romanze

notes: The parallel witness text begins on p. 374. Its Oxford column is mediated through Kaluza's transcript, which Bülbring compared again with Ashmole 61.

For the Edinburgh column, Treichel identifies Weber's 1810 edition, lists its inaccurate or altered readings with manuscript readings in parentheses, and omits Weber's modern terminal supplement. He does not identify how those manuscript readings were obtained, so direct Treichel autopsy is not asserted.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Englische Studien 22 (1896)
Internet Archive OCR for Englische Studien 22 (1896)
Treichel 1896 Sir Cleges supporting study (dissertation issue)

bibliographic.citation: Adolf Treichel, Sir Cleges: Eine mittelenglische Romanze (PhD diss., University of Leipzig, 1896).
bibliographic.title: Sir Cleges: Eine mittelenglische Romanze

notes: The BSB digital object preserves the dissertation introduction/study but not the journal issue's parallel Middle English text. It is therefore supporting scholarship with verified zero target-text overlap.
catalog_description is used provisionally because the current schema has no separate supporting-scholarship or monograph-study entity type; this entity is not a manuscript catalog record.

rights.component: The repository XML artifact as a complete digital file
rights.basis: No repository-level license file was found in the inspected checkout. The XML combines public-domain source text and edition content with a U-M digital encoding and embedded provider terms, so no blanket artifact license is inferred.

rights.component: The current U-M digital item and encoding in the United States
rights.basis: The current U-M item displays the RightsStatements.org No Copyright–United States designation.
notes: NoC-US is territorially limited and does not automatically resolve contract or provider-use restrictions.

rights.component: The availability statement embedded in the 2006 XML
rights.basis: The XML states ‘Where applicable, subject to copyright’ and warns that other restrictions on distribution may apply, without granting a redistribution license.
notes: This is a provider-term layer and not proof of ownership in the medieval texts or 1913 edition.

rights.component: The 1913 edition, including McKnight's editorial contributions and published typography
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1913, before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff of 1 January 1931.
notes: No worldwide conclusion is made.

rights.component: The anonymous medieval verbal work Dame Siriz
rights.basis: The work is an anonymous thirteenth-century Middle English composition; this conclusion concerns the abstract verbal work, not any manuscript photograph, catalog expression, or modern edition.

rights.component: The anonymous medieval verbal work Interludium de clerico et puella

editorial_practices.description: McKnight presents three principal humorous tales and appends the independent Interludium to Dame Siriz for comparison.
editorial_practices.scope: The four medieval texts represented by agd2855

editorial_practices.description: McKnight states that manuscript abbreviations are expanded while otherwise aiming at exact reproduction of manuscript forms.
editorial_practices.scope: Dame Siriz, Fox and Wolf, and Sir Cleges manuscript-based texts

editorial_practices.description: McKnight states that punctuation is normalized or supplied; the Dame Siriz headnote repeats that punctuation is supplied by the editor.
McKnight states that capitalization is normalized rather than reproduced diplomatically.
Proofs were collated with rotographic manuscript copies; earlier printed editions supplied comparison readings, and Sir Cleges was collated across its two witnesses.
editorial_practices.scope: Dame Siriz, Fox and Wolf, and Sir Cleges

editorial_practices.description: The U-M encoding includes the title page and work texts on printed pp. 1–59 while representing most other front and back matter only through omitted-section and page milestones.
editorial_practices.scope: U-M digital encoding and repository XML
notes: The encoding declaration states that OCR was uncorrected and no content editing was performed.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, agd2855.xml at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Identifies U-M item AGD2855.0001.001, McKnight's 1913 source book, 2006 OCR/Level 1 encoding practice, and the exact encoded selection structure.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Middle English Humorous Tales in Verse, AGD2855.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Current stable item page, public full-text access, bibliographic description, and No Copyright–United States rights designation.

evidence.citation: George H. McKnight, ed., Middle English Humorous Tales in Verse (Boston and London: D. C. Heath, 1913), complete scan.
evidence.locator: title page; printed pp. 1–59; complete volume
evidence.summary: Establishes the edition identity, four encoded texts, page ranges, editorial apparatus, and source notes.

evidence.citation: McKnight 1913, preface and introduction to the three principal tales.
evidence.locator: editorial-method statement at preface pp. v–vi / PDF pages 13–14; source-witness statements in the three tale introductions
evidence.summary: States that abbreviations are expanded, punctuation and capitalization normalized, and proofs collated against rotographic manuscript copies. McKnight names Digby 86 as the textual base for Dame Siriz and Fox and Wolf, Ashmole 61 as the Sir Cleges base, and the Edinburgh witness as the source of Sir Cleges lines 40–42 and 130–132. The preface does not identify the continuous transcript or compositor copy, so proof collation is not modeled as transcription from either rotographs or codices.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata and scan derivatives for middleenglishhum00mckn.
evidence.summary: Identifies the complete scan carrier and available PDF, OCR, OCR XML, and scan-data derivatives.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS Digby 86 catalog record and pinned TEI description.
evidence.summary: Dates the Worcestershire codex circa 1272–1282 and locates Fox and Wolf at ff. 138r–140r and Dame Siriz at ff. 165r–168r.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete 430-canvas facsimile of MS Digby 86.
evidence.locator: Fox and Wolf canvases 283–287; Dame Siriz canvases 337–343
evidence.summary: Confirms the two discontiguous work ranges inside the complete manuscript image sequence.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, MS Ashmole 61 catalog record and pinned TEI description.
evidence.summary: Dates the manuscript 1490–1510, identifies Rate/Rathe as scribe, and locates Sir Cleges at ff. 67v–73r.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian complete 358-canvas facsimile of MS Ashmole 61.
evidence.summary: Confirms the Sir Cleges range and that the work ends with Amen and a fish drawing partway down f. 73r before the next text begins on the same surface.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF attribution and rights metadata for MS Digby 86 and MS Ashmole 61.
evidence.summary: The exact manifests attribute the photographs to the Bodleian, apply CC BY-NC 4.0, and link Digital Bodleian terms.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.19.1.11 catalog record.
evidence.summary: Identifies the fifteenth-century manuscript, former shelfmark MS Jac. V. 7, 27, Sir Cleges at f. 71, missing terminal work leaf, and microfilm surrogates Mf.Sec.MSS.238 and 695.

evidence.citation: NLS IIIF Presentation 2 manifest for digital object 133475158.
evidence.summary: Describes a complete 370-canvas digital object and supplies exact image-service identifiers and the provider rights statement.

evidence.citation: NLS complete PDF and IIIF images for Adv.MS.19.1.11.
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact Sir Cleges range within the complete manuscript facsimile.

evidence.citation: Local integrity and render audit of the provider PDF for NLS Adv.MS.19.1.11.
evidence.summary: pdfinfo reads all 370 pages. pdftoppm rendered every page and separately rendered target pages 149–166. qpdf reports recoverable linearization hint-table/shared-object mismatches and exits 3 while stating that the operation succeeded with warnings. Provider bytes were retained without repair.

evidence.citation: NLS rights metadata for digital object 133475158.
evidence.summary: The exact object carries In Copyright – Rights-holder(s) Unlocatable or Unidentifiable and no open image license.

evidence.citation: British Library, Add MS 23986, Archives and Manuscripts record 032-002097619.
evidence.summary: Describes the vellum roll, its imperfect 84-line Interludium, date under Edward I, current ‘Mislaid’ status, and ‘MICROFILM 2026 AVAILABLE’ surrogate.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog footer rights statement.
evidence.summary: States that all catalog text is available under CC BY 4.0 except where otherwise stated.

evidence.citation: Wilhelm Heuser, ‘Das Interludium de Clerico et Puella und das Fabliau von Dame Siriz,’ Anglia 30 (1907): 306–319.
evidence.summary: Prints the 84-line Interludium anew from Add MS 23986, compares it with Dame Siriz, and rejects direct derivation of either surviving text from the other.

evidence.citation: Henry Weber, ed., Metrical Romances of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Centuries, vol. 1 (Edinburgh, 1810), xli, 329–354, 381.
evidence.locator: title page; Sir Cleges source statement at printed p. xli / PDF page 45; text at printed pp. 329–354 / PDF pages 423–446; variant and supplement note at printed p. 381 / PDF page 473
evidence.summary: Weber identifies the Advocates' Library paper manuscript as the only Sir Cleges copy known to him, prints its text, says the manuscript ending is defective, encloses his attempted supply in brackets, and states on p. 381 that printed lines 533–540 were added by the editor. This supports a manuscript-to-Weber transcription edge only for printed lines 1–532 and a separately scoped modern supplement.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata and scan derivatives for metricalromances01webeuoft.
evidence.summary: Identifies the complete 1810 volume 1 scan carrier, 480-page PDF, plain-text OCR, scan-data XML, and item metadata; the item metadata marks the volume NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.

evidence.citation: Adolf Treichel, ‘Sir Cleges. Eine mittelenglische Romanze,’ Englische Studien 22 (1896): 345–389.
evidence.locator: article pp. 345–389; source-method discussion pp. 360–361 / PDF pages 370–371; parallel text pp. 374–389 / PDF pages 384–399
evidence.summary: Prints parallel Oxford and Edinburgh texts. On pp. 360–361 Treichel identifies Weber's 1810 Edinburgh edition, lists Weber's inaccurate or altered readings with manuscript readings in parentheses, and says explicitly that he used an Ashmole transcript made by Max Kaluza and compared again with the original by Karl D. Bülbring. The source does not identify how Treichel obtained the Edinburgh manuscript readings.

evidence.citation: BSB digital object bsb11817459, Treichel's 1896 Sir Cleges dissertation issue.
evidence.summary: The complete 38-canvas object preserves Treichel's study but has verified zero overlap with the parallel Middle English text printed in the journal version.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright, Anecdota Literaria (London, 1844), complete scan.
evidence.locator: Dame Siriz, printed pp. 2–13 / PDF pages 17–29
evidence.summary: Prior Dame Siriz edition explicitly collated in McKnight's variant apparatus.

evidence.citation: Thomas Wright and James Orchard Halliwell, Reliquiae Antiquae, vol. 2 (London, 1845), complete scan.
evidence.locator: Fox and Wolf, printed pp. 272–278 / PDF pages 285–291
evidence.summary: Prior Fox-and-Wolf edition represented by McKnight's Wright siglum and comparison apparatus.

evidence.citation: Eduard Mätzner, Altenglische Sprachproben, vol. 1, part 1: Poesie (Berlin, 1867), BSB digital object bsb10747525.
evidence.locator: Dame Siriz pp. 105–113 / canvases 113–121; Fox and Wolf pp. 130–136 / canvases 138–144
evidence.summary: Prior texts collated in McKnight's variant apparatus; the correct poetry volume is distinguished from a false-lead dictionary component.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, records 46, 594, 1094, and 3093.
evidence.summary: Provides independent work identifiers, witness loci, conventional line counts, forms, and classifications for all four texts.
notes: DIMEV gives Sir Cleges witness counts of 564 lines in Ashmole and 532 in NLS, which conflict with McKnight's 570 and 531 witness counts and his 576-line composite text.

evidence.citation: BSB IIIF rights metadata for bsb11817459 and bsb10747525.
evidence.summary: Both exact BSB objects apply RightsStatements.org No Copyright – Non-Commercial Use Only 1.0.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Copyright Term and the Public Domain, Circular 15T.
evidence.summary: Current Copyright Office guidance states that works published before 1 January 1931 are in the U.S. public domain as of 2026.

open_questions.question: What continuous transcript or compositor copy supplied McKnight's Dame Siriz, Fox and Wolf, and Sir Cleges texts before their proofs were collated against the rotographs?
next_steps: Search McKnight's papers, publisher records, correspondence, and surviving production materials for transcripts, copy text, or compositor instructions.

Compare the three complete McKnight texts mechanically against prior editions and the manuscript facsimiles, separating continuous agreements from apparatus-only consultation before asserting any version_of or transcribes relation.
If the historical rotographs or marked proofs survive, inspect them for transcription, correction, and compositor annotations rather than inferring their function from the published wording alone.

open_questions.question: Did McKnight independently consult Add MS 23986 for the Interludium appendix, or did he reproduce Heuser's 1907 manuscript-based edition?
next_steps: Inspect McKnight's papers or correspondence for appendix preparation notes.
Compare every reading and bracketed restoration against Heuser and the BL microfilm if access is granted.

open_questions.question: What exactly does the British Library's ‘MICROFILM 2026 AVAILABLE’ surrogate cover, and can a research reproduction be ordered while the original roll remains mislaid?
next_steps: Submit the BL reproduction/reference form with shelfmark Add MS 23986 and catalog record 032-002097619.
Request confirmation of full-roll coverage, image quality, price, delivery format, and reuse terms.

open_questions.question: Do McKnight's rotographic manuscript copies survive, and if so where are they held?
next_steps: Search McKnight archival papers and Ohio State University finding aids.
Ask relevant archives whether rotographs or correspondence about the 1913 edition survive.

open_questions.question: How should McKnight's Sir Cleges witness counts (Ashmole 570; Edinburgh 531; composite 576) be reconciled with DIMEV's counts (Ashmole 564; NLS 532)?
next_steps: Audit physical, scribal, and editorial line positions in both complete facsimiles.
Separate supplied, omitted, rubric, and trailer lines before normalizing witness extents.

open_questions.question: How did Treichel obtain the Edinburgh manuscript readings that he used to correct Weber's 1810 print?
next_steps: Search Treichel, Kaluza, Bülbring, and Englische Studien correspondence or editorial papers for an Edinburgh collation or transcript.
Compare Treichel's correction list and E column mechanically against Weber 1810 and the complete NLS facsimile to characterize the intervention without inferring an undocumented material source.

open_questions.question: Does Kaluza's Bülbring-checked working transcript of the Ashmole Sir Cleges survive, and if so where is it held?
next_steps: Search archival descriptions for the papers of Max Kaluza, Karl D. Bülbring, and Adolf Treichel.
If the transcript is located, record its shelfmark, physical extent, corrections, and exact relationship to Treichel's Oxford column.

open_questions.question: On which exact NLS folios and lines do McKnight's supplied Sir Cleges lines 40–42 and 130–132 occur?
next_steps: Align McKnight's numbered text against NLS canvases 149–166 and Treichel's E column.
Add exact folio and line locators without changing the confirmed passage-scoped relation.
